title: Services, Units, Boot State, and Logs · Command Brief: Service Recovery
summary: Diagnose a service that is enabled but unavailable by separating desired boot state, current process state, dependencies, configuration, and logs. Planned artifact: Add and verify the linux service recovery increment in a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.
Inspect vendor units, drop-ins, environment sources, daemon reload requirements, and effective configuration.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Predict Service Recovery
steps.instruction:
theory case — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Service Recovery contract for the supplied data.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.

content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Service Recovery condition decided your prediction.

content.value: GNU Bash Reference Manual — Controls Bash parsing, expansion, quoting, redirection, pipelines, startup, job control, scripting, and error behavior.

steps.title: Bound Service Recovery

After tracing the worked code, choose the Service Recovery application whose preconditions and limits still match the supplied case.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.

content.text: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Capture identity, path, process, file, and service state; predict parsing and scope; run one safe simulated observation; interpret exit status; then change one bounded layer and verify rollback.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback. A defensible solution applies Service Recovery, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet.

content.code:
systemctl show lab-worker.service -p ActiveState -p SubState -p Result
journalctl -u lab-worker.service -b --since '10 minutes ago'
# Restart only after evidence identifies a reversible cause.
content.caption: Runnable safe terminal evidence example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Automatic infinite restart is a complete availability strategy. This fails the Service Recovery contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

steps.title: Defend the Service Recovery model
steps.instruction: Write the theory evidence note for Service Recovery: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 5. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.
